01

Project Overview

Objective: Analyse a logistics fleet database to identify the key drivers of truck maintenance costs and build a predictive model that classifies trucks as Deteriorating or Stable/Improving based on their 3-year cost trajectory. This project demonstrates a complete end-to-end data science workflow — SQL exploration through feature engineering to machine learning.
ML Goal
Binary Classification
Deteriorating vs Stable/Improving trucks
Target Variable
cost_trajectory
50/50 perfectly balanced split
Data Period
Jan 2022 – Dec 2024
36 months of fleet operations
Total Spend Analysed
$5.73M
Across all makes and repair types

Project Phases

PhaseDescriptionStatus
Phase 1SQL Exploration & EDA — 5 Focused Challenges✅ Complete
Phase 2Data Preparation & Feature Engineering🔄 Next
Phase 3ML Modelling — Classification & Regression⏳ Pending
Phase 4Results, Interpretation & Business Insights⏳ Pending

Dataset Overview

Key Tables Used

  • trucks120 trucks, 6 makes
  • truck_utilization_metrics3,312 monthly records
  • trips83,738 trip records
  • drivers, loads, routesSupporting tables

Fleet Composition

  • Freightliner565 records
  • Peterbilt573 records
  • Mack501 records
  • Volvo505 records
  • International436 records
  • Kenworth340 records
03

Challenge 1 — Maintenance Cost by Make & Type

Question: Which truck brands cost the most to maintain? Does maintenance type drive cost more than make?
Highest Avg Cost Make
Mack — $2,039
Per repair record
Lowest Avg Cost Make
Volvo — $1,864
Only $175 gap across all makes
Most Expensive Type
Tire — $2,054–$2,495
Consistently highest across all makes
Cheapest Type
Inspection — $479–$545
~4x cheaper than all other types
Average cost spread is only ~9% ($175) — make is a weak standalone predictor
Freightliner and Peterbilt together account for ~40% of total fleet spend
Downtime is nearly identical across all makes (24–25 hrs) — weak ML feature
Inspection column (white/light) stands out immediately — ~4x cheaper than all other types
ML Implication: Truck make shows only modest cost variation (~9% spread) and is unlikely to be a top predictor. maintenance_type is the strongest categorical signal — Inspection behaves as a completely separate cost class. It will be one-hot encoded in Phase 2. Downtime hours are nearly uniform and may be dropped after correlation testing.
04

Challenge 2 — High-Frequency Repair Trucks

Question: Which trucks have the most repeated maintenance events? Does repair frequency predict lifetime cost?
Most Repaired Truck
TRK00003 — 41 repairs
Peterbilt 2018 — $90,161 lifetime cost
High Frequency Tier
38 trucks (31.7%)
≥27 repairs — avg $60,213 lifetime cost
Medium Frequency Tier
56 trucks (46.7%)
20–26 repairs — avg $45,582 lifetime cost
Cost Gap High vs Low
$25,986
1.76x more expensive — strong signal
All Top 20 trucks show High Frequency tier — TRK00003 leads with 41 repairs
Recalibrated tiers: 31.7% / 46.7% / 21.7% — balanced and meaningful
Clean cost progression — each tier step adds ~$13K in lifetime cost
Key Decision — Tier Recalibration: Original thresholds (15+/8-14/<8) placed 98.3% of trucks in a single tier — useless for ML. Thresholds were recalibrated using percentile-based distribution (≥27 / 20–26 / <20) to create a balanced, meaningful three-tier split. This kind of critical thinking about features is what separates good data science from mechanical query running.
ML Implication: total_repairs as a raw numeric feature and repair_tier with recalibrated thresholds are both valuable. The $25,986 cost gap between High and Low tiers gives the model strong signal to learn from. Both versions will be tested in Phase 2.
05

Challenge 3 — Fuel Efficiency vs Maintenance Cost

Question: Do trucks with lower MPG incur higher maintenance costs? Is fuel efficiency an early signal of mechanical wear?
Highest MPG Make
Mack — 6.51 MPG
Best average across all trips
Fleet MPG Spread
Only 0.11 MPG
6.45–6.56 across entire fleet
MPG vs Cost Relationship
No meaningful link
Near-flat scatter trend line
ML Verdict
Likely Drop
Too narrow to carry signal
0.02 MPG spread across makes — essentially identical fuel efficiency
Near-flat trend line — no meaningful MPG/cost relationship exists
ML Implication: average_mpg carries only 0.11 MPG variation across the entire fleet — insufficient to carry predictive signal. The scatter plot confirms no relationship with maintenance cost. This feature will be included in the initial feature table but is expected to rank low and will likely be dropped after correlation testing in Phase 2.
06

Challenge 4 — Utilization Rate vs Maintenance Cost

Question: Do trucks that are worked harder incur higher maintenance costs? Is utilization rate a strong predictor?
Fleet Utilization Range
0.76 – 0.89
13.3% spread — reasonable variance
Highest Cost Month
October — $1,480–$1,720
Consistent spike across all 3 years
Lowest Cost Month
February — $912–$991
Consistently cheapest each year
U-Shaped Pattern Found
Medium tier cheapest
Both overworked and underworked trucks cost more
Weak positive trend — utilization alone is not a strong standalone predictor
U-shaped pattern — Medium utilization trucks cost least to maintain
ML Implication: utilization_rate shows a weak positive signal and will be included but is unlikely to be a top predictor. The U-shaped tier pattern suggests tree-based models (Random Forest, XGBoost) will handle this better than linear models. A new feature — month or season — should be engineered in Phase 2 as the October cost spike is a stronger and more consistent signal.
07

Challenge 5 — Maintenance Cost Over Time

Question: Are maintenance costs increasing year over year? Which trucks are deteriorating fastest? Can we identify a binary classification target?
Peak Spend Year
2023 — $1.97M
Dropped to $1.82M in 2024
Parts vs Labor Split
78% parts / 22% labor
Consistent across all 3 years
Most Deteriorated Truck
TRK00040 — +$2,217
2022 to 2024 cost increase
Fleet Trajectory Split
Exactly 50 / 50
60 Deteriorating vs 60 Stable — perfect balance
Parts dominate at 78% consistently — labor only 22% of total spend
Perfect 50/50 split — no class imbalance to handle in ML modelling
Engine and Transmission show consistent upward trend — Tire and Repair declining in 2024
Inspection averages ~$510 — approximately 4x cheaper than all other maintenance types
Key Finding — Convergence Pattern: Deteriorating trucks started cheaper in 2022 (avg $1,667) and ended more expensive in 2024 (avg $2,215). Stable trucks started more expensive ($2,188) and got cheaper ($1,724). Trucks that had expensive repairs early got properly fixed — while cheaper early repairs masked growing problems. This counterintuitive finding is worth highlighting in interviews.
ML Implication: cost_trajectory (Deteriorating=1, Stable=0) is our binary classification target variable. The 50/50 split means perfectly balanced classes — no resampling needed. cost_change_2022_to_2024 becomes a powerful engineered numeric feature. Engine and Transmission rising costs may be worth encoding as separate trend features.
08

Phase 1 Summary

Challenge 1

Cost by Make & Type

maintenance_type drives cost far more than make. Inspection is ~4x cheaper than all other types — the single strongest visual signal in Phase 1.

Challenge 2

Repair Frequency

Fleet is uniformly high-frequency. Original thresholds were recalibrated using percentile-based distribution to create meaningful tiers with a $25,986 cost gap.

Challenge 3

Fuel Efficiency

MPG variation is only 0.11 across the entire fleet — too narrow to carry predictive signal. No relationship with maintenance cost confirmed by scatter plot.

Challenge 4

Utilization Rate

Weak positive signal. U-shaped tier cost pattern discovered — medium utilization trucks cost least. Seasonal month pattern is a stronger finding.

Challenge 5

Cost Over Time

Perfect 50/50 Deteriorating vs Stable split identified. Cost trajectory becomes the binary classification target. Engine and Transmission costs trending upward.

09

ML Features for Phase 2

Feature Table

FeatureTypeSource TableExpected Importance
cost_trajectory Binary Target maintenance_records 🎯 Target Variable
maintenance_type Categorical maintenance_records 🔴 High
cost_change_2022_to_2024 Numeric (engineered) maintenance_records 🔴 High
total_repairs Numeric maintenance_records 🔴 High
repair_tier Categorical (ordinal) maintenance_records 🟠 Medium
truck_make Categorical trucks 🟠 Medium
truck_age Numeric (engineered) trucks 🟠 Medium
utilization_rate Numeric truck_utilization_metrics 🟠 Medium
month / season Categorical (engineered) truck_utilization_metrics 🟠 Medium
avg_downtime_hrs Numeric maintenance_records 🟡 Low — test then drop
average_mpg Numeric trips Drop — confirmed weak
10

What's Next — Phase 2 Roadmap

Step 1
Master Feature Table
Join all tables into one flat dataframe — one row per truck
Step 2
Data Cleaning
Handle TRK00011 NaN, nulls, outliers
Step 3
Feature Engineering
Encode categoricals, create truck_age, season, cost_change
Step 4
Train / Test Split
80/20 stratified split — random state 42
Step 5
Baseline Model
Logistic Regression → Random Forest → XGBoost
Step 6
Feature Importance
Validate Phase 1 hypotheses — does maintenance_type lead?
Key Questions Phase 2 Will Answer: Does maintenance_type emerge as the top predictor as expected? Does average_mpg rank low as predicted? Does the seasonal month pattern add meaningful signal? Can we achieve >75% accuracy on a 50/50 balanced dataset with the features identified here?
